segments.s467-g73: نگون شد is a two-word predicate: نگون (a verbal/adjectival stem meaning 'fallen/overthrown') plus شد (past/state‑change marker 'became/was'), so together mean 'was overthrown/was struck down'. In this poetic line the subject سر ("head") follows the predicate (verb‑subject order is common in classical Persian poetry). نامبردار modifies شاه (nominal modifier before the noun here, with the ezāfe link omitted in poetry), so سر نامبردار شاه = 'the head of the aforementioned/said king'.
segments.s467-g74: ازو is the prepositional/compound form of از + او ('from him'), placed before the verb. بازگشتند is past tense, third person plural ('they returned/withdrew'). یکسر modifies سپاه ('the whole/entire army'); word order here places the prepositional phrase and verb before the subject (ازو بازگشتند یکسر سپاه = 'from him returned the whole army' → 'the whole army withdrew from him').
segments.s467-g65: سکندر is the subject (a proper name). سپارد is the past tense of سپردن (to entrust/deliver). The indirect object is expressed after the verb with the preposition phrase به ما (to us), and کشوری is the direct object (a realm/country) placed after the verb, producing a S‑V‑(IO)‑O order typical of classical Persian.
segments.s467-g66: بدین پادشاهی is a prepositional phrase meaning “by/with this kingship” (بدین = ب- + this), functioning as a circumstantial phrase. شویم is the present/subjunctive form of شدن (here 'that we may become'), introducing the desired/result state; افسری is a predicate noun meaning 'bearers of the diadem' (an epithet for crowned rulers). In poetry بدین is a fused form used instead of modern با این/به این.
segments.s467-g67: همی رفت is a habitual/continuous past construction (همی + past verb) meaning 'was going/went on'; با او 'with him' follows the verb as a prepositional accompaniment. دو دستور اوی is a quantified noun phrase where دو (two) precedes دستور (here the title/office 'dastur', 'commander/officer') and the enclitic اوی marks possession ('his').
segments.s467-g68: که is a relative pronoun 'who/that' linking to the preceding noun phrase. دستور بودند is a predicative clause with بودند as past plural of بودن 'they were' (reports their office), and و گنجور اوی adds a coordinated appositive/attribute 'and (were) his gankhûr/treasury (keepers)', with اوی again the possessive enclitic 'his'.
segments.s467-g69: This is a coordinate locative listing: مهین بر چپ = 'Mahin on (the) left' where بر marks location. و links the second coordinate; ماهیارش (ماهیار + ش) is the name Mahyar with the possessive enclitic -ش 'his' (i.e. 'his Mahyar'), and به راست places that person 'to the right' with به + راست ('to the right').
segments.s467-g70: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; شب تیره شد is a clause 'the night became dark' with شد (past of شدن) as the verb. از هوا باد خاست is a simple clause 'a wind rose from the air' where از indicates origin/source and خاست (archaic past 'arose') is the verb. The sequence is a prototypical temporal clause followed by its result/action.
segments.s467-g71: یکی is an indefinite subject/adverbial 'one/a certain (person)'. دشنه is the direct object 'a dagger'. بگرفت is the past tense 'he seized / took up'. جانوشیار follows as an appositive proper name identifying the actor; in poetry apposition after verb/object is common.
segments.s467-g72: بزد is the past tense 'he struck'. The preposition بر marks the target(s) of the blow; here the line shows two adjacent بر forms introducing two targets (poetic repetition/parallelism) yielding 'upon the head and (upon) the chest of the king'. و coordinates the two targets, and سینه شهریار is 'the king's chest' with شهریار as the possessed noun.
segments.s467-g49: گرانمایگان is a plural noun (literally 'the valued/illustrious ones') serving as the subject. زینهاری is used predicatively — a noun/adjectival noun meaning 'admonition/warning' — and شدند is the 3rd person plural past of شدن, here meaning 'became' or 'were made'; together زینهاری شدند = 'were admonished' or 'came to be in the state of admonition'.
segments.s467-g50: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; اوج بزرگی is a genitival phrase 'the apex/height of greatness' (اوج = apex, بزرگی = greatness). به governs the following noun as a direction/state 'into' or 'to' and خواری is the resulting state 'humiliation/abasement'; شدند is again the 3rd pl. past of شدن, so the whole clause means 'they became (reduced) from the height of greatness into humiliation.'
segments.s467-g51: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; دارا here names the person (literally 'the wealthy/possessor', used as a proper name); چنان is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying دید 'saw' (3rd sg. past). برگاشت is a transitive past verb 'lifted/turned' (here 'turned') and روی is its object 'face' — so برگاشت روی = 'he turned his face'.
segments.s467-g52: گریزان is a present-participial adjective 'fleeing' describing the subject; همی is the archaic narrative/progressive particle (here giving a continuous or iterative sense, as in 'kept on / was ...ing'); رفت is the 3rd sg. past 'went' so together 'گریزان همی رفت' = 'he fled/kept fleeing'. با‌های هوی is a phrase of accompaniment: با = 'with', های here marks the plural/collective quality of the exclamation, and هوی is an interjection ('hoy!'/a shout); thus 'با‌های هوی' = 'with cries of “hoy!”'.
segments.s467-g53: برفتند is the 3rd person plural past 'they set out / departed'. با شاه is a prepositional phrase 'with the king' indicating accompaniment. سیصد سوار is the noun phrase 'three hundred horsemen' (the numeric phrase functions as the subject/agent of برفتند). Word order places the verb first (typical in epic narrative) followed by accompaniment and the group description.
segments.s467-g54: از ایران is a locative/source phrase 'from Iran'. هرانکس is an indefinite relative 'whoever/anyone', followed by the relative particle که 'who/that'. بد نامدار is a descriptive compound: بد qualifies نامدار; taken together here it describes the kind of people recruited — 'of (ill or strong) repute' (in context functioning as 'famed/notorious'). The relative clause modifies هرانکس: 'whoever was (so) named/known.'
segments.s467-g55: دو دستور here refers to two 'dasturs' (a title/office — commanders or leaders); بودش is the past third-person clitic form meaning 'he had' or 'there was to him' (بود + enclitic -ش). گرامی is an adjective 'honored/esteemed' qualifying the following معرفه 'دو مرد' — the phrase repeats/clarifies the number and persons: 'two esteemed men.'
segments.s467-g56: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. با او = 'with him' (accompaniment). بدندی is the past plural verb agreeing with 'دو مرد' — in this poem it functions as an archaic/poetic past meaning 'were with / joined' (i.e., they accompanied him into combat). به دشت نبرد is a locative phrase 'to/into the plain of battle' (به + دشت نبرد) specifying where they accompanied him. Overall: 'two men who accompanied him to the field of battle.'
segments.s467-g33: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. اسکندر is the proper name 'Alexander' as the subject. آگاه شد is a past simple verbal phrase 'became aware' (آگاه + شد, شد = become); زین سخن is a prepositional/ablative phrase 'from these words' (زین = 'from this' in poetic usage, سخن = 'word/speech'). Word order is topical: the time/clause-marker چو + subject + verb + source phrase.
segments.s467-g34: که begins a subordinate clause ('that' / 'which') giving the content of what Alexander learned. دارای is the participial/adjectival form 'having/possessing' modifying دارا (here indicating the possessor/rich one). چه is the interrogative 'what', افگند is the past tense 'threw/cast' (literal 'cast down'), and بن is the noun 'root/foundation' — together: 'what the possessor (Darā) had cast down the foundation.' The clause is a free indirect object of 'آگاه شد'.
segments.s467-g35: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 3rd‑person verb 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a subordinate (purpose/result) clause 'that/so that'. برکشیدند is a past plural verb 'they lifted/raised' (object-subject agreement); نای is the direct object 'ney' (reed flute). The structure = 'he ordered that they raise the ney.'
segments.s467-g36: غو is a noun 'uproar/tumult' presented as an exclamation or scene-setting noun. کوس 'war-drum' is the instrument; برخاست is a past intransitive 'rose/arose' (the drum sounded/was raised). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. هندی is an adjective/noun 'Indian' qualifying the following noun; درای is an adjectival/attributive form 'wealthy/possessing' (here likely qualifying the host or contingent). Word order is compressed and poetic: scene‑setting noun + instrument + verb, then conjunction + descriptor.
segments.s467-g37: بیامد is simple past 'came'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. اصطخر here is a place name (Istakhr) as source. چندان is a quantifier/adverb 'so much/so great' modifying سپاه 'host/army' (which is the noun). The phrase describes the vast number of troops that arrived.
segments.s467-g38: که introduces the result clause 'so that'. خورشید is 'the sun' as subject. بر is the preposition 'on/upon'. چرخ here means 'the sky/heaven'. گم کرد راه is a poetic verb phrase 'lost its way' — گم (lost) + کرد (past auxiliary/verb 'made/did') with راه (way) as the object; word order is inverted for poetic effect ('گم کرد راه' = 'را ه گم کرد' in prose).
segments.s467-g39: برآمد is past 'arose/issued forth'. خروش 'cry/roar' is the noun of sound; سپاه 'army/host' is its possessor or agent ('the army's cry'). از دو روی literally 'from two faces/sides' = 'from both sides' (روی used as 'side'). The phrase describes the sound issuing from both flanks.
segments.s467-g40: بی‌آرام شد is a copular construction 'became restless' (بی‌آرام = restless; شد = became). مردم جنگجوی is a noun phrase 'the warlike/warrior people' where جنگجوی postmodifies مردم (poetic adjective after the noun); together they form the subject that becomes restless.
segments.s467-g57: This short nominal clause introduces a single person: 'یکی موبدی' uses یکی in the indefinite/specific sense 'a certain/one' followed by the noun موبدی (mobed, Zoroastrian priest). نام او ماهیار is a verbless appositive: نام + pronoun او + the name ماهیار, literally 'his name (was) Mahyār.' Classical Persian often omits the copula 'بود' in such name-introductions.
segments.s467-g58: دگر مرد را is 'the other man' with را marking definiteness/emphasis; نام جانوشیار is the parallel appositive 'his name (was) Jānūshyār.' The sequence mirrors the first clause, again omitting the copula and giving the second person's name.
segments.s467-g59: چو دیدند begins a temporal subordinate clause: چو = 'when' and دیدند is past tense 'they saw.' کان here functions as a subordinating particle 'that'/'which' introducing the content clause; کار بی‌سود گشت is a copular construction where گشت 'became' (past) takes the predicate بی‌سود 'fruitless' describing کار 'the affair/matter.'
segments.s467-g60: This coordinate nominal phrase states the result: بلند اختر و نام دارا گذشت. بلند اختر 'the lofty/high star' is a poetic epithet; و links the second item نام دارا where نام functions as 'name' or 'fame' and دارا here refers to Dārā (presented as an epithet/name). گذشت is the past 'passed'/'waned' describing what happened to both the star and the name/fame.
segments.s467-g61: یکی با دگر گفت is a common conversational formula: 'one (of them) said to the other.' گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' (classical narrative style). کین شوربخت is an address or comment on the subject — شوربخت 'unfortunate/wretched' modified by the demonstrative/particle کین (used here to point to 'this/that wretch').
segments.s467-g62: ازو دور شد is a short verbal clause meaning 'was removed from him' or 'departed from him' (ازو = 'from him', دور شد = 'became distant/was taken away'). افسر و تاج و تخت lists the items lost: افسر 'helmet', تاج 'crown', تخت 'throne'; the coordinating و simply links these nouns as the things removed.
segments.s467-g63: بباید زدن is a periphrastic obligation: بباید = 'one must/it is necessary' followed by the infinitive/masdar زدن 'to strike' and the object دشنه‌ای 'a dagger.' بر برش places the action onto the (victim's) body; here بر + برش indicates 'against him/on him' (poetic economy of expression for 'into/at him').
segments.s467-g64: وگر introduces an alternative 'but if'/'or else.' تیغ هندی 'an Indian sword' is the instrument; یکی is indefinite 'one (should)...' and بر سرش is the prepositional phrase 'upon his head' (بر + سر + suffix ش = 'on his head'). The clause offers the second means of execution.
segments.s467-g17: دگر گفت = 'another said' (دگر here = 'another' or 'one more'); گفت is the verb 'said' (past), with the subject omitted because the speaker is indefinite/previously introduced. کای is the vocative particle 'O' placed before the addressee; مهتر هندوان is a nominal phrase 'chief (مهتر) of the Indians (هندوان)', with the title followed by the group it governs.
segments.s467-g18: A string of epithets coordinated by و: 'خردمند و دانا و روشن‌روان' = 'wise and learned and bright‑souled'. In Persian adjectives and descriptive nouns follow (or are placed after) the vocative/title they modify; روشن‌روان is a poetic compound epithet ('bright‑soul, clear‑minded'). There is no copula — the adjectives directly describe the addressed chief.
segments.s467-g19: همانا is an emphatic particle 'truly/indeed'. که introduces the content clause ('that/that which follows'). نزد تو آمد خبر is literal 'near you came news' = 'news came to you'; نزد + pronoun marks 'in the presence of / to you', آمد is the verbal predicate 'came/arrived', and خبر is the item that arrived (news/report). Note the verb precedes the noun phrase for emphasis (‘خبر آمد’ construction).
segments.s467-g20: This is an indirect question clause introduced by که: 'که ما را چه آمد ...' = 'what has come to us ...' (i.e. what has befallen us). ما را uses را to mark the experiencer/affected party ('to us' / 'upon us'); چه is the interrogative 'what'; آمد here has the sense 'befell/occurred' rather than simple 'come'; ز اختر به سر is a prepositional phrase 'from the star upon the head', indicating the astrological source or cause.
segments.s467-g21: سکندر (Alexander) is the subject. بیاورد is the verb 'bring/bring about' used here with a future/prospective sense ('will bring' or 'shall bring'); لشکر is the direct object 'army/host'; ز روم is a prepositional phrase 'from Rome/the Romans' (here meaning Byzantium/Eastern Rome). Word order is Subject–Verb–Object–PP.
segments.s467-g22: A negative result clause introduced by نه ... نه (see next sentence) but here condensed: برماند ('remain, be left') is used impersonally with ما را ('for us') marked by را to show the affected party: 'nothing will remain for us'. آباد بوم is a nominal phrase 'inhabited/prosperous dwelling/land' (آباد modifying بوم). The verb expresses persistence/remaining (برماند).
segments.s467-g23: A coordinated negative list begun by نه: 'نه پیوند و فرزند و تخت و کلاه' = 'no bonds (family ties) and children and throne and hat/crown'. Items are joined by و ('and'); the initial نه negates the whole coordinated phrase. تخت و کلاه are paired royal symbols (throne and headgear/crown).
segments.s467-g24: Continuation of the negation: 'نه دیهیم شاهی نه گنج و سپاه' = 'neither rural/local kingship nor treasure and army'. دیهیم qualifies شاهی as 'rural/local' (a provincial or village‑based rule), and گنج و سپاه is a final coordinated pair negated by the preceding نه. The double نه ... نه pattern enumerates separate things that will be absent.
segments.s467-g41: سکندر is the subject (proper name). به آیین صفی is an adverbial prepositional phrase of manner ('in the manner/rite/array of ...') placed between subject and verb; classical Persian allows this flexibility. برکشید is a past‑tense verb (3rd person singular) meaning here ‘drew (a weapon)’; the verb is transitive but the object is left implicit for poetic compression.
segments.s467-g42: This is two short predications linked without a conjunction. هوا نیلگون شد uses شد (past of شدن) as a copula 'became' — noun + adjective + شد = 'the air became indigo.' زمین ناپدید omits an explicit copula and reads as a verbless predicate 'the earth (was) vanished/was lost,' a common poetic ellipsis.
segments.s467-g43: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when.' دارا functions as the subject (a name/title in context) and بیاورد is the past-tense verb 'brought'; classical Persian allows the object (لشکر) to follow the verb. به راه is a locative phrase 'onto the road/on the march' modifying بیاورد.
segments.s467-g44: سپاهی is the subject 'the soldiers' and نه negates the following predicative phrase. بر here introduces the domain/reason and functions like 'for/on (account of)' (classical usage). آرزو رزمخواه is a noun + modifier: آرزو 'desire' qualified by رزمخواه 'seeking battle/war‑desirer' — together indicating 'not war‑seekers out of mere desire.'
segments.s467-g45: شکسته‌دل is an adjectival phrase 'broken‑hearted.' و is a simple connective. گشته is the perfect participle/verb 'had become' (here past resultative), and از رزم سیر is a prepositional phrase 'sated/weary from battle' — together: 'and had become, from battle, sated/weary.'
segments.s467-g46: سر بخت ایرانیان is a genitive phrase 'the head/forefront (سر) of fortune (بخت) of the Iranians' — a poetic way to mean 'the Iranians' fortunes/leadership.' گشته is again 'became/was turned' and زیر follows as an adverbial/predicative particle meaning 'under/low' or 'subject to' — so the whole clause means their fortunes were brought low/subjugated.
segments.s467-g47: نیاویختند is a negative past (3rd pl.) of آویختن 'to cling/attach' meaning 'they did not attach/ally.' ایچ (ایچ/ایچ) is an emphatic negative 'at all' placed after the verb for emphasis. با رومیان is a simple prepositional complement 'with the Romans.'
segments.s467-g48: چو is 'when.' روبه شد is a verbal phrase: روبه (here functioning as 'toward') plus شد 'became/turned,' so together 'turned toward/was turned into.' آن دشت شیر ژیان is a noun phrase 'that plain of lion‑life' (شیر ژیان is a poetic genitive: 'life/kind of lions' = 'haunt/realm of lions'), so the clause reads roughly 'when that plain turned toward/the realm of lions.'
segments.s467-g25: ار introduces a conditional ('if'). ایدونک is an archaic/emphatic particle meaning ‘such/so’ and qualifies باشی; باشی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive form 'you be' (classical/poetic). مرا is the object 'me' (here split as م + را in the word list) with را marking the person affected, and یارمند is a compound noun/adjective 'helpful, an ally'. The whole segment means 'If you are such a helper to me.'
segments.s467-g26: که introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that' / 'that'). از خویشتن is a prepositional phrase 'from/of oneself' (خویشتن here reflexive: 'myself'). بازدارم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive 'I restrain/will keep off', taking گزند ('harm') as its object at the end; literal order is 'from myself restrain I harm', i.e. 'that I may ward off harm from myself.'
segments.s467-g27: فرستمت is past transitive with the enclitic object 'ت' = 'I sent you'. چندان modifies گهرها as a measure/adverb ('so many' / 'to such an extent'), گهرها is the plural 'jewels', and ز گنج ('from the treasure') indicates source; the order is typical poetic SOV/adjacency with the measure word before the noun.
segments.s467-g28: کزان ('from which') refers back to the jewels just mentioned; پس = 'after/thereafter' introduces the result clause. نبینی is the negative present/subjunctive 2nd-person 'you will not see/experience' (verb precedes the pronoun in classical order), and از گنج رنج is a compact phrase 'from (lack of) treasure—suffering', i.e. 'you will not thereafter experience want of wealth.'
segments.s467-g29: همان here is an emphatic adverb ('that very / then / indeed') placed before the locative phrase. در جهان نیز = 'in the world as well'. نامی شوی uses نامی (famous) as a predicative adjective and شوی (2nd-person 'you become') as the copula/verb; the classical order places the predicate before or next to the verb 'become'.
segments.s467-g30: به نزد بزرگـان = 'in the presence of / before the great ones'; گرامی شوی = 'you will be honored/esteemed' with گرامی as predicative adjective and شوی again the 2nd-person copula. The preposition نزد governs بزرگان (the great), and the whole phrase states the social recognition you will receive.
segments.s467-g31: هیونی is a plural/collective noun 'beasts/animals' (here treated as the objects scattered). برافگند is a past 3rd-person verb 'he/they threw off, scattered'. بر سان باد is a simile 'like the wind' (بر سان = 'like/as', باد = 'wind'), so the clause literally reads 'he scattered the beasts as the wind (does)'.
segments.s467-g32: بیامد is past 'came' (3rd person). بر + the following nominal string marks the manner: فور (here 'a sudden surge') and فوران ('outpouring/eruption') intensify one another, and نژاد is 'race/lineage/offspring'; together they mean 'a sudden gushing forth of a race (or progeny) came.' The word order is poetic but reads naturally as 'there came a sudden outpouring of offspring.'
segments.s467-g1: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. آن is the demonstrative 'that' pointing to the letter; پاسخ‌نامه is the object 'reply/answer-letter'. دارا is a proper name (Dara) serving as the clause's subject. بخواند is a third-person past/historic-present narrative form meaning 'he read' or 'he read aloud'.
segments.s467-g2: ز marks cause/relation here ('at/because of' or the comparative 'than') and introduces the phrase کار جهان 'the affairs/ways of the world'. در شگفتی is a prepositional phrase 'in astonishment', and بماند is the third-person singular narrative/present of ماندن 'remain' meaning 'he remained / was left'.
segments.s467-g3: سرانجام is an adverb 'at last / finally'. گفت is the narrative past 'said' introducing reported speech. این refers to the preceding matter ('this'); ز + کشتن introduces the standard old Persian comparison 'than killing', and بتر is the comparative 'worse' (so: 'this is worse than killing').
segments.s467-g4: که here introduces the content of what was said ('that'). من is the first-person subject 'I'; پیش رومی means 'before/in the presence of the Romans' (پیش = before/in front of); ببندم is a first-person singular subjunctive/present form of بستن 'to bind' used here in a volitional sense 'I will gird'; کمر 'waist/girdle' is the object of the action ('gird my loins').
segments.s467-g5: ستودان (praise) is the subject/impersonal theme here. مرا is the object 'to me' (the construction is idiomatic: '... آید' = 'pleases me / is better for me'). بهتر آید literally 'better comes' = 'is better / pleases more'. ز ننگ is the comparative 'than shame'.
segments.s467-g6: یکی is 'a certain' (introducing an indefinite agent). داستان زد uses زد in a literary sense 'uttered / told' (verb follows the thing told). برین = بر + این ('upon/at that'); مرد سنگ 'stone-man' (literally 'man of stone') is a descriptive noun phrase meaning a hard or unmoved man—so 'a certain tale struck/affected that stony man.'
segments.s467-g7: که‌گر is an archaic/composite conditional 'if'. آب دریا is a genitive phrase 'the water of the sea'. بخواهد رسید combines the modal/auxiliary خواه + the verb رسیدن: here it expresses a wish/intention or hypothetical attempt 'were to want/try to reach'.
segments.s467-g8: درو is a contraction of در او 'in it' (referring back); قطره باران 'a drop of rain'. نیاید is the negative present/subjunctive 'would not come / would not appear' (literary negative), and پدید means 'appear/come into being'. Together: 'in it not a drop of rain would appear.'
segments.s467-g9: همی بودمی is an archaic/perfective expression meaning 'I was / I used to be' with همی adding continuous/emphatic sense ('always'). The verb (بودمی) comes before its complement (یار هرکس) in this poetic order: 'I was an ally of whoever (هرکس) in battle.' به جنگ is a simple prepositional phrase 'in/at war/battle' modifying 'یار'.
segments.s467-g10: چو شد = 'when it became' (چو = 'when'). The phrase مر مرا is an emphatic/archaic doubling of the first‑person object ('to me'); م here is an archaic/existential/copular fragment used with شد, and را is the object/topic marker attached after the pronoun. زین نشان = 'because/of this sign (of the saddle)' (زین = saddle), and کار تنگ = 'the affair became tight/limited' (کار = affair; تنگ = narrow/difficult). Overall: 'When, on account of this saddle‑token, the matter became constrained for me.'
segments.s467-g11: نبینم is negative present/gnomic 'I do not see' (here functioning like 'I see no…'). همی again gives an emphatic/continuous sense ('ever/at all') so the phrase means 'I do not (ever) see in the world any friend/person.' در جهان locates the negation 'in the world.'
segments.s467-g12: This is an inverted existential clause: 'بجز ایزدم نیست فریادرس' = 'There is no rescuer except my god.' بجز introduces the sole exception (except), ایزدم = 'my deity/Izad', نیست negates existence, and فریادرس is the predicate noun 'rescuer/succor'. The order emphasizes the exception (ایزدم).
segments.s467-g13: چو = 'when'; یاور نبودش is 'there was no helper' (نبودش = 'was not' / 'his/its absence'), and ز نزدیک و دور means 'from (i.e. neither) near nor far' — a poetic way to say 'neither nearby nor at a distance.' The clause sets the situation (no aid available anywhere).
segments.s467-g14: یکی نامه بنوشت = 'someone wrote a letter.' یکی here is the indefinite 'someone/a certain one.' بنوشت is a poetic/archaic perfect of 'to write' (he wrote). نزدیک فور is an adverbial phrase 'to the near one / immediately to someone nearby' (نزدیک = near, فور = immediately), so the whole segment means 'a man at once wrote a letter to a nearby person.'
segments.s467-g15: پر از ... = 'full of ...' (پر = full, از = 'of/from'). لابه (here: lament/entreaty), زیردستی (submission/subservience) and درد (sorrow/pain) are listed as the emotional state. This participial/prepositional phrase modifies the following main clause, describing the speaker's/his condition.
segments.s467-g16: نخست = 'first.' آفرین here is an exclamation/noun 'praise' or 'acclamation' (not the verbal stem), بر جهاندار کرد = 'he directed (made) [it] to the sovereign' — کرد is the past of کردن 'to do/utter.' In short: 'First he uttered praise to the ruler.'

sentences.s467-s37: The head of the aforementioned king was struck down, and the entire army withdrew from him.
sentences.s467-s33: Alexander entrusted a realm to us, that by this kingship we might become bearers of the diadem.
sentences.s467-s34: He went with him two of his officers—who were commanders and his treasurers.
sentences.s467-s35: Mahin on the left and (his) Mahyar on the right; when night grew dark, a wind rose from the air.
sentences.s467-s36: A certain man seized a dagger — Janushiyār struck at the head and the king's breast.
sentences.s467-s25: The noble ones were admonished; from the peak of greatness they fell into humiliation.
sentences.s467-s26: When Dâra saw this, he turned his face and, fleeing, went off with cries of 'hoy!'
sentences.s467-s27: Three hundred horsemen departed with the king — from Iran, whoever was famed.
sentences.s467-s28: He had two honored commanders — two men who had been with him in the field of battle.
sentences.s467-s17: When Alexander became aware from these words of what Dārā the possessor had cast down the foundation.
sentences.s467-s18: He commanded that they raise the ney; tumult — the war-drum arose, and the Indian contingent of the wealthy (host)...
sentences.s467-s19: From Istakhr came such a host of troops that the sun lost its way upon the heavens.
sentences.s467-s20: The army's cry rose from both sides; the warlike people became restless.
sentences.s467-s29: One was a mobad named Mahyār; the other's name was Jānūshyār.
sentences.s467-s30: When they saw that the matter had become fruitless, Dara's lofty star and fame passed.
sentences.s467-s31: One said to the other, 'This unlucky man has been deprived of helmet, crown, and throne.'
sentences.s467-s32: We must drive a dagger at him, or else (use) an Indian sword upon his head.
sentences.s467-s9: Another said, "O chief of the Indians, wise and learned and bright‑souled."
sentences.s467-s10: Truly, news has come to you of what has befallen us from the star upon our head.
sentences.s467-s11: Alexander will bring an army from Rome; nothing of our land will remain inhabited.
sentences.s467-s12: No kin or children, no throne or crown; no rural kingship, no treasure or army.
sentences.s467-s21: Alexander, arrayed in martial fashion, drew his weapon; the air turned indigo and the earth vanished.
sentences.s467-s22: When Dārā brought his host onto the road, the soldiers were not, for the sake of desire, eager for battle.
sentences.s467-s23: Broken‑hearted and grown weary of fighting, the Iranians' fortune was brought low.
sentences.s467-s24: They did not at all attach themselves to the Romans; when that plain turned toward the life of lions.
sentences.s467-s13: If you be such a helper to me that I may keep harm away from myself.
sentences.s467-s14: I have sent you so many jewels from the treasure that thereafter you will not suffer for lack of wealth.
sentences.s467-s15: Thereupon you will also become famous in the world, and you will be honored before the great.
sentences.s467-s16: He scattered the beasts like the wind; at once there came a sudden outpouring of a (new) race.
sentences.s467-s1: When Dara read that reply, he remained astonished at the affairs of the world.
sentences.s467-s2: At last he said, 'This is worse than killing; I will gird my loins before the Romans.'
sentences.s467-s3: Praise pleases me more than shame; a certain tale struck that stony man.
sentences.s467-s4: If the sea's water wished to reach it, not a drop of rain would appear in it.
sentences.s467-s5: I was always an ally to anyone in battle; when this saddle‑token befell me, my affairs became constrained.
sentences.s467-s6: I see no friend in the world; there is no rescuer except my deity.
sentences.s467-s7: When no helper was there, neither near nor far, a man immediately wrote a letter to someone nearby.
sentences.s467-s8: Full of entreaties, subservience and sorrow, he first offered praise to the sovereign.
